does: This paper empirically investigates whether higher interpretability in Sparse Autoencoders correlates with better model steering utility in large language models. The authors train 90 SAEs across three LLMs using five architectures and six sparsity levels, then evaluate interpretability using SAEBench and steering utility using AxBench. Through rank-agreement analysis using Kendall's tau-b coefficient, they find only a weak positive association (τ_b ≈ 0.298) between interpretability and steering performance, directly challenging the assumption that more interpretable SAE features necessarily enable more effective behavior control. This finding suggests that interpretability alone is insufficient for predicting steering effectiveness.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature using Sparse Autoencoders for mechanistic interpretability of LLMs, which emerged from earlier research on dictionary learning and feature decomposition in neural networks. It extends prior efforts to use SAEs for model steering and control by critically examining assumptions underlying this approach. The paper responds to the implicit premise in recent SAE-based steering work—that interpretable features automatically translate to utility—and engages with broader mechanistic interpretability research questioning whether interpretability metrics reliably predict downstream task performance. It also draws on established evaluation frameworks like SAEBench and introduces novel comparison methodologies (rank coefficient analysis) to systematically assess claimed relationships between interpretability and functionality.
enables: By revealing the interpretability-utility gap, this work opens new directions for identifying which features truly steer LLM behavior versus merely being interpretable artifacts. The finding that not all interpretable features are equally effective for steering motivates development of better feature selection criteria and steering mechanisms that go beyond interpretability scores. This could lead to improved SAE architectures or hybrid approaches combining interpretability with direct steering efficacy measures. The work also enables more rigorous evaluation frameworks for mechanistic interpretability research generally, suggesting that future interpretability work should validate utility assumptions empirically rather than assuming interpretability implies downstream benefits, potentially reshaping how interpretability research is evaluated across the field.
